items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.q: Why is ice-albedo feedback a positive feedback loop?
items.correct: Less ice means less reflective surface, so more sunlight is absorbed, causing more warming and more melting.
items.misconception: Because warming is positive (a good thing).
items.random_wrong: Because polar bears help spread heat.
items.why: Students confuse 'positive' (mathematics) with 'good' (everyday). Positive feedback amplifies the change, in any direction - a negative outcome can result from a positive feedback loop.
The answer examiners want is: Soil pH. “Predators” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An organism's role in an ecosystem is its?
items.why: It's Niche. “Habitat” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many trophic levels does a typical food chain have at minimum?
items.why: The real figure is 3 levels. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
